items.title: Crystal LSP implementation for Windows and Linux
items.author: /u/No_1944
items.content: Since Crystalline only supports some POSIX platforms, I wrote an LSP implementation which works cross platform.

Why the first time run spec with -O1 is so slow?(15 min on a very little shard) I almost think about crystal spec is in the dead loop.

items.title: Vibe-coding in Crystal
items.author: renich
items.content:
I know vibe-coding is, at the same time, a popular and unpopular topic among programmers. Many see it as some new kind of zero-code programming, others as a powerful “autocompletion on steroids” tool.

In any case, I’d like to know what people here think about it. If you have used it and which problems have you faced.

I’ve done it a lot, honestly. I have many projects in Crystal that I’ve tried to develop using opencode.ai and gemini-cli.

My first paint point is that Crystal is not a mainstream language yet. That makes it complicated. Especially to use idiomatic crystal with agents.

That said, I have some projects that have “taken off” meaning that they actually work, even if I use them myself only. Others have been just impossible to code.

So, what’s up? Have you tried doing this? Is vibe-coding the devil in disguise? What have you stumbled upon?

items.title: Announcing CrymbleUI - a modern, native Crystal GUI framework
items.author: wolfgang371
items.content:
ever missed a Crystal native GUI framework? Maybe you want to check out CrymbleUI.

A nice and fast GUI framework for Crystal. Declarative and reactive. Its name is a pun of Crystal and nimble CrymbleUI’s first line of code emerged 2.11.2025, 19:46.

Currently with SFML backend and for Linux. But easily portable to e.g. Windows.

items.title: RFC: Standard Image Types in Stdlib
items.author: naqvis
items.content:
Add standard image types (colors, pixel formats, color spaces) to Crystal’s standard library to provide a common foundation for the image processing ecosystem.

This RFC is meant to start a discussion about standardizing image types in Crystal. If the community finds value in this approach, anyone interested can help move it forward.

The Problem: Ecosystem Fragmentation

Crystal’s image ecosystem is fragmented. Each library defines its own incompatible types:

struct RGBA
  property r, g, b, a : UInt16
end

module Color
  struct RGBA
    property r, g, b, a : UInt8
  end
end

struct Color
  property r, g, b, a : UInt8
end
  • Developers must learn different APIs for each library
  • No shared understanding of color representation
  • Unclear semantics (premultiplied? color space? bit depth?)
  • Difficult to build tools that work across libraries
  • Each library reinvents basic concepts

Comparison to Other Languages

- Standard library provides foundation:

// In stdlib
package image/color

type RGBA struct {
    R, G, B, A uint8
}

type RGBA64 struct {
    R, G, B, A uint16
}

All Go image libraries use these types.

- Community standard via crate:

pub trait Pixel {
    type Subpixel: Primitive;
    const CHANNEL_COUNT: u8;
}

pub struct Rgba<T>([T; 4]);
pub struct Rgb<T>([T; 3]);

Most Rust image libraries implement these traits.

- PIL/Pillow provides foundation:

from PIL import Image

# Standard modes: "RGB", "RGBA", "L" (grayscale), etc.
img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height))
pixel = img.getpixel((x, y))  # Returns (r, g, b) tuple

Most Python image libraries interoperate via PIL’s standard modes.

- Java stdlib provides foundation:

// In java.awt
BufferedImage img = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_INT_ARGB);
int rgb = img.getRGB(x, y);  // Standard packed ARGB format

Kotlin and Android use or .

A that defines:

  1. Standard color types with clear semantics
  2. Standard pixel format definitions
  3. Standard buffer interface
  4. Standard color space definitions

This allows libraries to:

  • Share a common vocabulary
  • Build on a solid foundation

When all libraries use the standard foundation, you have consistency:

require "image"  # Standard foundation

# All libraries use the same color types
lib_a_color = Image::RGBA8.new(255, 0, 0, 255)
lib_b_color = Image::RGBA8.new(255, 0, 0, 255)

# Same type everywhere
lib_a_color == lib_b_color  # true

Build on the foundation instead of reinventing:

require "image"

module MyImageLib
  class Canvas
    @pixels : Slice(Image::RGBA8)
    
    def [](x, y) : Image::RGBA8
      @pixels[y * @width + x]
    end
  end
end

Proposed Standard Library Addition

Add module to Crystal stdlib (or create minimal shard):

module Image
  # 8-bit RGBA (premultiplied alpha)
  struct RGBA8
    getter r : UInt8
    getter g : UInt8
    getter b : UInt8
    getter a : UInt8
    
    def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 255_u8)
    end
    
    # Premultiplied alpha semantics
    # Valid range: 0 <= r,g,b <= a <= 255
  end
  
  # 8-bit RGBA (non-premultiplied alpha)
  struct NRGBA8
    getter r : UInt8
    getter g : UInt8
    getter b : UInt8
    getter a : UInt8
    
    def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 255_u8)
    end
  end
  
  # 16-bit RGBA (premultiplied alpha)
  struct RGBA16
    getter r : UInt16
    getter g : UInt16
    getter b : UInt16
    getter a : UInt16
    
    def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 65535_u16)
    end
  end
  
  # 16-bit RGBA (non-premultiplied alpha)
  struct NRGBA16
    getter r : UInt16
    getter g : UInt16
    getter b : UInt16
    getter a : UInt16
    
    def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 65535_u16)
    end
  end
  
  # 8-bit RGB (no alpha)
  struct RGB8
    getter r : UInt8
    getter g : UInt8
    getter b : UInt8
    
    def initialize(@r, @g, @b)
    end
  end
  
  # 8-bit grayscale
  struct Gray8
    getter y : UInt8
    
    def initialize(@y)
    end
  end
  
  # 16-bit grayscale
  struct Gray16
    getter y : UInt16
    
    def initialize(@y)
    end
  end
end

module Image
  # Standard color spaces
  enum ColorSpace
    # sRGB (IEC 61966-2-1) - default for most images
    SRGB
    
    # Linear RGB - for compositing and blending
    LinearRGB
    
    # Adobe RGB (1998) - wider gamut
    AdobeRGB
    
    # Display P3 - modern wide gamut displays
    DisplayP3
  end
end

module Image
  # Describes pixel encoding in memory
  enum PixelFormat
    RGBA8      # 4 bytes: R, G, B, A (premultiplied)
    NRGBA8     # 4 bytes: R, G, B, A (non-premultiplied)
    RGB8       # 3 bytes: R, G, B
    GRAY8      # 1 byte: Y
    
    RGBA16     # 8 bytes: R, G, B, A (premultiplied, little-endian)
    NRGBA16    # 8 bytes: R, G, B, A (non-premultiplied, little-endian)
    RGB16      # 6 bytes: R, G, B (little-endian)
    GRAY16     # 2 bytes: Y (little-endian)
    
    def bytes_per_pixel : Int32
      case self
      in .rgba8?, .nrgba8? then 4
      in .rgb8? then 3
      in .gray8? then 1
      in .rgba16?, .nrgba16? then 8
      in .rgb16? then 6
      in .gray16? then 2
      end
    end
    
    def premultiplied? : Bool
      case self
      in .rgba8?, .rgba16? then true
      else false
      end
    end
  end
end

module Image
  # Axis-aligned rectangle
  struct Rectangle
    getter min : Point
    getter max : Point
    
    def initialize(@min, @max)
    end
    
    def width : Int32
      max.x - min.x
    end
    
    def height : Int32
      max.y - min.y
    end
  end
  
  struct Point
    getter x : Int32
    getter y : Int32
    
    def initialize(@x, @y)
    end
  end
end

Buffer Interface (Minimal)

module Image
  # Minimal interface for pixel buffers
  # Libraries can implement this or provide their own richer APIs
  module Buffer
    abstract def width : Int32
    abstract def height : Int32
    abstract def pixel_format : PixelFormat
    abstract def color_space : ColorSpace
    
    # Optional: direct buffer access
    # abstract def to_unsafe : Pointer(UInt8)
    # abstract def stride : Int32
  end
end
  1. : Only essential types, not operations
  2. : Explicit about premultiplication, bit depth, endianness
  3. : Different types for different formats
  4. : Libraries can add their own types

What This Does NOT Include

  • Image I/O (PNG, JPEG, etc.) - libraries handle this
  • Image operations (resize, filter, etc.) - libraries handle this
  • Format conversion - libraries handle this
  • Drawing primitives - libraries handle this

This is - the building blocks.

Libraries can adopt incrementally:

: Use standard color types internally

# In any library
alias RGBA = Image::RGBA16  # Use standard type

: Expose standard types in API

def [](x, y) : Image::RGBA16

: Implement standard interfaces

class Canvas
  include Image::Buffer
end
  1. : Adds types to standard library
  2. : Existing libraries must migrate
  3. : Doesn’t cover all pixel formats
  4. : Makes choices about representation

Why in stdlib (or minimal shard)?

  • Always available, no dependencies
  • Stable API, semantic versioning
  • Community standard by default
  • Like Go’s package

Minimal shard alternative:

  • Can be adopted before stdlib inclusion

Why these specific types?

: Most common formatsPremultiplied vs non-premultiplied: Both needed, different use cases: Prevents confusion

Alternative 1: Do nothing

Let ecosystem remain fragmented.

: Problem will worsen as more libraries emerge

Alternative 2: Pick one library as standard

Make everyone use one specific library’s types.

: Creates monopoly, not neutral

Alternative 3: Comprehensive image library

Build full-featured stdlib image library.

: Too large for stdlib, limits innovation

Alternative 4: Just protocols/interfaces

Define interfaces without concrete types.

: Doesn’t solve the “every library has different RGBA” problem

package color

type Color interface {
    RGBA() (r, g, b, a uint32)
}

type RGBA struct {
    R, G, B, A uint8
}

: All Go image libraries use these types

pub trait Pixel {
    type Subpixel;
    const CHANNEL_COUNT: u8;
}

pub struct Rgba<T>([T; 4]);

: De facto standard in Rust ecosystem

from PIL import Image

# Standard image modes
img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height))
img = Image.new("RGBA", (width, height))
img = Image.new("L", (width, height))  # Grayscale

: Standard modes used across Python image ecosystem

public class Color {
    public Color(int r, int g, int b, int a)
}

: Standard in Java ecosystem

  1. Stdlib or separate shard?

    • Stdlib: More authoritative, always available
    • Shard: Faster iteration, easier adoption
    • Current proposal: RGBA8, NRGBA8, RGBA16, NRGBA16, RGB8, Gray8, Gray16
    • Missing: CMYK, YCbCr, HSV, LAB - add later?
    • Enum sufficient or need more metadata?
    • Assume little-endian or make explicit?
    • Include in foundation or leave to libraries?
  1. How to encourage library adoption?
  2. Migration guide for existing libraries
  3. Documentation and examples
  1. Image operations (resize, filter, etc.)
  2. Format I/O (PNG, JPEG readers/writers)
  3. Color space conversion algorithms
struct CMYK8
struct YCbCr8
struct HSV
struct LAB

module Image
  def self.convert(color : RGBA8, to : ColorSpace) : RGBA8
end

module Image
  class SimpleBuffer
    include Buffer
    # Reference implementation
  end
end

module Image
  def self.register_format(name : String, reader : FormatReader)
end

But these are - start with minimal foundation.

10 posts - 4 participants

items.title: Jelly VM - A BEAM clone, maybe better
items.author: /u/Fabulous-Repair-8665
items.content:
Wrote it back in the May when I wanted to dive deeper into Erlang, continued it recently and turned out great, all it needs now is a programming language.

It is stack based, supports fault tolerance primitives and lightweight processes, a lot more flexible and comfortable to debug than BEAM.

It is not "battle tested" at all, that is up to you :P
